pattern: Sorting the whole array first makes every window of three consecutive elements as tight as possible, so if any such window still exceeds the allowed gap, no other grouping can do better either.
hints: Once the array is sorted, the three closest values to each other are always consecutive in the sorted order -- spreading them apart into different groups can only widen the gaps.
Sort the array, then chop it into consecutive chunks of three: the first three sorted values form one group, the next three form another, and so on.

For each chunk of three, the difference between its largest and smallest value is exactly (index + 2) minus (index) in the sorted array; the moment any chunk fails that check, no valid split exists at all, so return an empty array.

edge_inputs.description: nine values split cleanly into three valid triplets
four duplicate values force an oversized pair together, so no split works
minimum array size, exactly one triplet at the edge of the allowed gap
minimum array size, gap exceeds k by exactly one
all elements identical, always splittable regardless of k
only the smallest and largest allowed values appear, each in matching triples
sorted-consecutive grouping is required even though a naive index-order grouping would fail

stress.description: large random array with a mid-sized k, exercising both feasible and infeasible splits
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size - (size % 3)
    n = max(3, n)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 100000)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: large array constructed to always be feasible: values grouped into tight clusters of three
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size - (size % 3)
    n = max(3, n)
    nums = []
    for _ in range(n // 3):
        base = rng.randint(1, 99000)
        nums.extend([base, base + rng.randint(0, 3), base + rng.randint(0, 3)])
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    return [nums, 5]
